metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = the Schrodinger wave (with i) that propagates coherently; CRIMSON = the diffusion version (without i) that spreads irreversibly
metadata.note: Exclusions: no plausibility-argument derivation of the full equation, no Hamiltonian formalism, no i in complex numbers from scratch, no interpretation of what psi is

beats.narration_text: An electron, released in free space. Watch what happens next.

card.headline: Why the i in Schrodinger's Equation Changes Everything

beats.narration_text: Two equations. Nearly identical. One describes a wave — it travels, breathes, stays sharp. The other describes heat: it spreads and never comes back.
The only difference between them is a single letter. Remove the i from Schrodinger's equation and the quantum world stops being a wave.
One imaginary unit. Why does it decide whether quantum mechanics is even possible?

card.headline: Two equations differ by one factor of i. One propagates a wave. One spreads heat. Why does that single symbol decide whether quantum mechanics works at all?

beats.narration_text: The diffusion equation governs heat, ink in water, any spreading process. A blob of heat placed here moves outward and never contracts. That is irreversible.
A classical particle thrown in a direction stays on that trajectory. A reasonable person would expect a quantum particle to do the same.

props.headline: A classical particle thrown in a direction stays on that trajectory.…

beats.narration_text: But if you write the Schrodinger equation without the i, the electron packet dissolves instantly. No trajectory. Just a smear that widens forever.
So what does the i actually do?
Without the i, both the time derivative and the space derivative pull the wavefunction in the same direction — they add and push amplitude outward. That is diffusion.
The i rotates the time-derivative contribution ninety degrees in the complex plane. Now the two terms interfere instead of adding. The amplitude oscillates and travels.
That rotation keeps the total probability exactly one — the i is the keeper of the Born rule.

card.headline: The i keeps probability from leaking away

beats.narration_text: Without the i, probability leaks out of any region. Atoms could not be stable. Interference fringes could not form. Spectral lines would not exist.
The i is not an algebraic convenience. It is what makes quantum mechanics physically possible.
A worked example with illustrative numbers.

card.headline: An electron in a 1 nm pocket

beats.narration_text: Release an electron from a 1-nanometer pocket. Diffusion version: width hits 10 nanometers in 3 femtoseconds — gone. Schrodinger version: a coherent pulse crosses a 5-nanometer gap and arrives at a detector intact. Same packet. One letter makes the difference.
Remove the i: wave becomes heat, spreading forever. Keep it: amplitude propagates coherently. One symbol. That is why quantum mechanics exists.

card.headline: Remove the i: wave becomes heat, spreading forever. Keep it: amplitude propagates coherently. The i is why quantum mechanics exists.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics A Companion Guide series.
